articles.full_text:
Bengal: Schools shift to morning classes amid heatwave
Kolkata: Schools across several districts of West Bengal have begun shifting to morning classes after the School Education Department allowed institutions outside the hill areas to reschedule academic sessions for the next two weeks in view of prevailing heat conditions.
In a memorandum issued on June 1, the department authorised District Inspectors of Schools (Secondary and Primary Education) in all districts except the hill areas to permit schools to conduct classes in the morning “as per their convenience” for the next two weeks. The department said the decision was in line with existing practice during severe heatwave or hot weather conditions and was aimed at protecting students’ health without affecting their academic interests.
The department also asked district authorities to inform schools in advance so that teachers and students could adjust their schedules accordingly.
Following the directive, district education authorities in several districts, including Purulia, Jhargram, Murshidabad, Nadia, Howrah, Purba Medinipur, Birbhum and Uttar Dinajpur, issued follow-up orders permitting or directing schools to conduct classes in the morning shift for the next two weeks.
While most districts left the timings to individual institutions, some prescribed specific schedules. Jhargram District Primary School Council directed primary and junior basic schools to function from 6 am to 10.30 am, with Saturday classes ending at 8.30 am.
Purulia fixed the arrangement from June 3 to June 16 for all primary and junior basic schools, while Birbhum’s secondary education authorities asked schools to submit proposals for conducting morning sessions before implementation.
The Bengal Primary Teachers’ Association welcomed the decision, saying it had long demanded morning classes during periods of intense heat. The organisation urged the state government to provide seasonal fruits, oral rehydration solution (ORS) and adequate numbers of fans in schools.
The association also called for improved infrastructure in schools with tin or asbestos roofs and sought adoption of heat-mitigation measures in classrooms. Anand Handa, the association’s general secretary, said conducting classes in the morning shift was preferable to extending summer vacations.
